flashcards.front: What is the purpose of <fstream> header in C++?
flashcards.back: The <fstream> header provides classes for file input/output operations, including ifstream, ofstream, and fstream for reading, writing, and both reading/writing files respectively.

flashcards.front: What are the three main file stream classes in C++?
flashcards.back: ifstream (input file stream - for reading), ofstream (output file stream - for writing), and fstream (for both reading and writing).

flashcards.front: What does the file open mode 'ios::app' do?
flashcards.back: Append mode - data is added to the end of the file without modifying existing content. The file pointer is positioned at the end before each write operation.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between ios::trunc and ios::app modes?
flashcards.back: ios::trunc (default) deletes existing file content when opened for output. ios::app preserves existing content and appends new data at the end.

flashcards.front: What is seekg() function used for?
flashcards.back: seekg() positions the get pointer (input pointer) in a file stream. It allows random access reading from a specific position in the file.

flashcards.front: What is seekp() function used for?
flashcards.back: seekp() positions the put pointer (output pointer) in a file stream. It allows random access writing to a specific position in the file.

flashcards.front: What do tellg() and tellp() return?
flashcards.back: tellg() returns the current position of the get pointer (reading position). tellp() returns the current position of the put pointer (writing position). Both return a streampos object representing the byte offset.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between text mode and binary mode file opening?
flashcards.back: In text mode, newline characters are translated to/from platform-specific representations. In binary mode, data is written/read exactly as stored in memory without any translation.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of eof() function?
flashcards.back: eof() returns true when the end-of-file indicator is set, typically after attempting to read past the end of the file. Used to check if file reading has reached the end.

flashcards.front: How do you check if a file opened successfully?
flashcards.back: Use the is_open() member function which returns true if the file was successfully opened, or check if the stream object evaluates to true in boolean context.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between ifstream, ofstream, and fstream constructors?
flashcards.back: ifstream can accept filename directly and open in input mode. ofstream can accept filename and open in output mode. fstream requires specifying ios::in and/or ios::out modes explicitly.

flashcards.front: What does the ios::ate mode do?
flashcards.back: At end mode - opens the file and positions the file pointer at the end of the file. Unlike ios::app, it allows reading from anywhere and writing at any position (not just appending).

flashcards.front: What is streampos type in C++ file handling?
flashcards.back: streampos is a type used to store positions in stream buffers. It is returned by tellg() and tellp() and used as parameter for seekg() and seekp(). Typically equivalent to a long integer.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of close() function in file handling?
flashcards.back: close() disconnects the stream object from the physical file, flushing any buffered data and releasing system resources associated with the file.

flashcards.front: What happens if you don't explicitly close a file in C++?
flashcards.back: The file is automatically closed when the stream object goes out of scope (destructor is called), but explicit close() is recommended for clarity and error handling.
